## What Is Economics, and Why Is It Important

- Economics
	- Study of how humans make decision in the face of scarcity
- Scarcity
	- Human wants for goods, services and resources exceed what is available

### Introduction to FRED

- US Bureau of Labor Statistics
- FRED database
	- St. Louis Federal Reserve Bank's FRED database
	- Domestic and international variables over time
		- Mony, banking, and finance
		- Population, employment, and labour markets
		- National accounts (GDP), flow of funds, and international accounts
		- Production and business activity
		- Prices and inflation
			- Consumer Price Index
			- Producer Price Index
			- Employment Cost Index
		- International Data
		- US Regional data
		- Academic data (Penn World tables and NBER Macro-history database)


### The Problem of Scarcity
- Division and specialization of labour
	- A production innovation by Adam Smith

### The Division of and Specialization of Labour

- Formal study of economics began with Adam Smith (1723-1790)
	- The Wealth of Nations
- Division of labour
	- The way one produces a good of service is divided into a number of tasks that different workers perform

### Why the Division of Labour Increases Production

- Sub-dividing tasks helps workers and businesses produce a greater quantity of output
- Specialization
	- A small job allows workers to focus on the parts of the production process where they have an advantage
	- Workers who specialize in tasks often learn to product more quickly and with higher quality
- A business that focuses on one or a few produce (core competency) is more successful than organizations that have a wide range of products
- Economies of scale
	- For many goods, as the level of production increases, the average cost of producing each individual unit declines

### Trade and Markets

- Specialization only make sense, if workers can use the pay they received for doing their jobs
- Specialization requires trade


## Microeconomics and Macroeconomics

- Microeconomics
	- Focuses on the actions of individual agents within the economy
		- Households
		- Workers
		- Businesses
- Macroeconomics
	- Focuses on the economy as a whole
		- Growth of production
		- Unemployed people
		- Inflationary increase in prices
		- Government deficits
		- Levels of exports and imports


### Microeconomics

- Micro decision of individual businesses are influenced by whether the macro-economy is healthy
- Consumer behaviour
- Theory of the firm
- Markets for labour and other resources

### Macroeconomics
- Depends on the micro-economic decision that individual households and business make
- Growth in the standard living, low unemployment, low inflation
- Use of government macroeconomic policy to pursue goals
- Monetary policy
	- Policies that affect bank lending, interest rate, and financial capital markets
- Fiscal policy
	- Government spending and taxes
- Congress and the executive branch
	- Originates the federal budget

## How Economics Use Theories and Models to Understand Economic Issues

- John Maynard Keynes (1883-1946)
	- Economics is not just a subject area, but also a way of thinking
- Analyze issues and problems using economic theories that are based on assumptions about human behaviour
- Theory
	- A simplified representation of how two or more variables interact with each other
	- Abstract representation
- Model
	- Applied or empirical representation


- Circular flow diagram
	- Economy as consisting of two groups
		- Households
		- Firms
- Goods and services market
- Labour market

<img src="/images/Pasted image 20260401165228.png" alt="image" width="500">


- Firms produce and sell goods and services to households
- Households pay for goods and services

Household => demands goods
Firms => supply goods

Households => supply labour
Firms => demand labour


## How to Organize Economies: An Overview of Economic Systems

- Traditional economy
	- The oldest economic system and us used in parts of Asia, Africa, and South America
	- Occupations stay in the family
	- Little economic progress or development
- Command economy
	- Devoted to goals passed down from a ruler or ruling class
	- Ancient Egypt
	- Government decides what goods and services will be produced and what prices it will change for them
	  Government sets wages
	- Cube, North Korea
	- Centralized structure
- Market economy
	- Decentralized structure
	- Combines buers and sellers
	- Market economics are based on private enterprise
		- Private individuals or groups of private individual own and operate the means of production
		- Supply is based on demand
		- A person's income is based on their ability to convert resources (labour) into societal value
	- Market forces determine economic decisions
- Most economies are command and market

### Regulations - The Rules of the Game

- Markets and government regulations are entangled
- No such thing as a absolute free market
- Regulations are defined by the economy
- Market economies have few regulations
- Command-oriented economies operated using markets
- Underground economies (black markets)
	- Heavily regulated economies
	- Transactions without government approval

### The Rise of Globalizaion

- Globalization
	- Expanding cultural, political, and economic connections between people around the world
	- Increased buying and selling, services, and assets across national borders
	- International trade and financial capital flows
- Reasons for globalization
	- Improved shipping
	- Reduced transportation costs
	- Computing and telecommunications
	- Valuable produces and services can take the form of information
- Exports
	- Goods and services that one produces domestically and sells abroad
- Imports
	- Goods and services that one produces abroad and then sells domestically
- GDP (Gross domestic product)
	- Measures the total production in an economy
	

![What Is GDP? | Capital One](data:image/png;base64,)


- Export/GDP ratio has risen
- US economy is below the global average
	- Large economies can contain more of the division of labour inside their national borders
- Pushback against globalization
	- Political sovereignty
	- Increased economic inequality
